focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

By the following dusk the camp had learned a new way to be polite.

Nobody told Mina to stay clear of the kitchen. Nobody had to. The main house carried sound too well for that kind of order to survive as an order; it would have become gossip before the sentence was finished. Instead the place had gone careful around her. A pot of rice sat on the stove with the lid tipped so steam could get out. Two fillets of jack were wrapped in foil on a tray, one gone, one waiting. Someone had moved her mug from the drainboard to the far end of the counter, beside the can of instant coffee nobody claimed unless they were furious or broke. Through the screen wall the spoonbills kept up their raw nesting racket in the darkening trees, and under that noise the stilts complained every time the wind leaned.

The argument from the night before had not stopped ringing just because everybody had run out of things to throw at it.

Tomas's silence had been the loudest part. He had not shouted after the first few ugly minutes. He had gotten worse than that. He had gone exact. He had named fuel, hours, and the shape of his own suspension as if he were reading his losses off a county form. He had named his body too, which had been harder to hear. Mina had stood there with the annex logbook on the table between them and understood too late that proof could strip skin faster than lies did. Rhea had not rescued her. Rhea had also not let Tomas turn the whole thing into a neat charge sheet with Mina as sole defendant. That had been its own kind of mercy, and it had not felt kind.

All day Mina had been given work that kept her useful and away from the guest-facing side of camp. She had scrubbed the bat-room sink with diluted bleach until her knuckles smelled like a public pool. She had...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...d the shell path under the camp, her shirt was pasted to her skin and the gloves were full of sweat. She peeled them off finger by finger and stuffed them into an outer pocket of the sack. The boardwalk above her threw striped shade across the mud. Somebody in the kitchen dropped a pan. Beto laughed at something, then coughed hard enough to bend double, the sound carrying through the slats. Ordinary camp life was still going above her in all its frayed particulars: lantern batteries, donor calls, audit fallout, Rhea in the bat lab with her neat hard questions, Tomas somewhere trying to imagine nine thousand dollars of debt without a county paycheck under it, and Imogen looking at a half-corrupted hydrophone file and seeing possible rescue.

Mina put one hand on the bottom rung of the ladder to the boardwalk and stopped there.

With her palm pressed hard against the rung, she understood what she had actually been asking the eel to do. Not innocence. She was not stupid enough to ask the creek for that. She had wanted cleaner evidence, a correction sharp enough to lever her life a few inches off disgrace, something real and beautiful and wild enough, or at least not provably hers enough, to do the work confession never had. She had wanted discovery to rearrange blame into something survivable.

The logbook killed that fantasy on the page where live material entered outgoing flow.

The badge killed the rest.

She kept her hand on the rung until the aluminum bit a ladder pattern into her palm. Then she climbed. The dry sack dragged at her shoulder, and inside it the recovered badge kept tapping the logbook, plastic on paper, her name keeping time against BrightCurrent's storm ledger. She crossed the boardwalk without looking toward Imogen's office and headed for the bat lab.
